author: Thomas W. Brink, MD
executive_summary: Dr. Thomas Brink, a retail investor, vehemently opposes a proposed SEC rule that would allow corporations to shift from quarterly to semi-annual financial reporting. He argues this change would create an informational asymmetry that benefits corporate insiders and wealthy elites at the expense of ordinary individual investors.

positions.transparency: strongly opposes any reduction in corporate reporting transparency; favors maintaining quarterly reporting requirements

economic_arguments: Reducing reporting frequency from quarterly to semi-annual creates informational advantages for corporate insiders
Insiders would be able to profit from non-public knowledge during extended reporting gaps
The rule disproportionately benefits wealthy and elite market participants over ordinary investors
competition_arguments: Semi-annual reporting would create an uneven playing field between insiders and retail investors
Reduced disclosure frequency entrenches advantages of those with privileged access to corporate information
retail_investor_arguments: Individual investors would be harmed by reduced access to timely financial information
The proposed rule favors elite and insider interests over the average retail investor
Retail investors rely on frequent disclosures to make informed investment decisions
recommended_actions: Reject the proposed rule to allow semi-annual financial reporting
Maintain existing quarterly reporting requirements to protect retail investors
key_quotes: I write to vehemently oppose the proposed regulation that would allow corporations to report financial information every six months instead of every three months.
This change will allow insiders to determine how the markets will perceive corporations and profit from the knowledge that they have to the exclusion of individual investors.
In other words, 'The rich get richer' and the poor man suffers.
This proposed rule is purely for the elite.
